Paul opens this chapter not with gentle persuasion but with a direct charge: you have been bewitched. The Galatians have seen Christ crucified with their own eyes—not physically, but through the gospel proclamation. Yet somehow they have turned away from Him toward a false gospel centered on keeping the law. What makes this so piercing is that Paul is not accusing them of abandoning Christ entirely, but of returning to a principle they should have already rejected. They received the Spirit through faith, not through works of the law. They have experienced God's power and miracles among them. So why would they now try to finish what the Spirit began by relying on human effort and obedience to the law? The question stings because the answer should be obvious: they cannot. This is not a matter of degree or improvement—it is a fundamental reversal of how salvation actually works.

The logic Paul builds is unrelenting. Abraham believed God, and that faith was credited to him as righteousness. This is not a principle unique to Abraham or to the Old Testament—it is how God has always justified people. The law came 430 years later and could never alter that foundation. The law reveals sin and confines us under it, but it cannot give life. Anyone who relies on keeping the law stands under a curse, because the law demands complete obedience, and no one can deliver it. Only Christ can—He alone absorbed that curse when He hung on the tree, becoming a curse for us. This is not poetic language; it is the heart of redemption. Christ redeemed us so that the blessing promised to Abraham would reach the Gentiles, so that we might receive the promise of the Spirit through faith. The law was never meant to save; it was a guardian, a tutor to drive us to Christ, to show us our need for Him.

What should shake the Galatians awake is this: they have already experienced what salvation by faith looks like. They have the Spirit. They have been justified. And now they are being tempted to undo it all by adding human works to what Christ has finished. But Paul makes clear that the inheritance—the blessing, the promise, the life itself—comes through faith in Christ, not through the law. This was always God's plan. The promise to Abraham pointed to one seed: Christ. In Him, the dividing lines that seemed so important are erased. There is no distinction between Jew and Greek, slave and free, male and female. All who are baptized into Christ, all who have clothed themselves with Him through faith, are sons of God and heirs of the promise. The Galatians must recognize what they already are in Him, and refuse the lie that they need to add anything to their faith. The curse has been borne. The blessing is theirs. The Spirit is theirs. They are His.
